narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 42—higher than the typical applicant's 36.7—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is slightly below the average of 3.21. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted an 80% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the decision against your application. This high failure risk led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on that critical exam right away. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a 1 compared to the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about consistent early academic performance in a demanding environment. While your stronger-than-average LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset those lower decile rankings. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often rank in the middle or upper halves of their classes from the start, your early and later school standings deviated significantly downward, making the model cautious. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted preparation—but the model weighs these patterns heavily based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek bar prep resources early or explore part-time options, though full-time is common for 93% of students. Reapplying after strengthening those academic deciles, perhaps with additional test prep or GPA-boosting experiences, positions you well for approval next time. Your LSAT strength is a great foundation—build on it.
